flashcards.front: Define Binary Search Tree (BST) and state its fundamental property.
flashcards.back: A Binary Search Tree is a binary tree where each node has at most two children, with the key in the left subtree always less than the root's key, and the key in the right subtree always greater than the root's key. This property enables efficient O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion operations on average.

flashcards.front: Explain the BST Insert algorithm with pseudocode.
flashcards.back: Starting from the root, compare the new key with the current node: if smaller, go left; if larger, go right. Repeat until reaching a null position, then insert the new node as a leaf. Time Complexity: O(h) where h is tree height.

flashcards.front: Explain BST Delete operation with three cases.
flashcards.back: Case 1: Leaf node - simply remove it. Case 2: One child - replace node with its child. Case 3: Two children - find inorder successor (smallest in right subtree) or inorder predecessor (largest in left subtree), replace node's value, then delete the successor/predecessor recursively.

flashcards.front: Write and explain iterative BST Search algorithm.
flashcards.back: Node* search(Node* root, int key) { while(root != NULL && key != root->key) { if(key < root->key) root = root->left; else root = root->right; } return root; } This algorithm traverses only one branch, avoiding unnecessary comparisons.

flashcards.front: Compare time and space complexities of BST operations.
flashcards.back: Average Case: Search, Insert, Delete - O(log n); Space - O(h) for recursion stack. Worst Case (skewed tree): O(n) for all operations; Space - O(n). Balanced BST (AVL/Red-Black): O(log n) guaranteed for all operations.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Inorder Predecessor and Inorder Successor in BST?
flashcards.back: Inorder Successor is the smallest node in the right subtree (or nearest ancestor where node is in its left subtree). Inorder Predecessor is the largest node in the left subtree (or nearest ancestor where node is in its right subtree). Both are used in BST deletion when deleting a node with two children.

flashcards.front: State advantages and limitations of BST.
flashcards.back: Advantages: Efficient search (O(log n) avg), ordered traversal, simple implementation. Limitations: Performance degrades to O(n) with unbalanced trees, requires balancing (AVL, Red-Black trees) for guaranteed efficiency, no built-in self-balancing in standard BST.

flashcards.front: Applications of Binary Search Trees in real-world scenarios.
flashcards.back: Used in database indexing (B-trees foundation), symbol tables in compilers, priority queues, auto-complete features, and maintaining sorted data with fast lookup. Also used in Huffman coding trees and implementing sets/multisets in C++ STL.
